coredata.dc:description: The objective of this research is to examine how various cooling and lubrication techniques affect the machinability of Duplex F53 steel when turning it, with particular attention to residual stress, surface roughness, chip morphology, tool wear, machining temperature, and crystallographic structures. According to the microstructure analysis, the presence of additional austenite grains at high temperatures during dry machining results in an increase in hardness. On the other hand, cooling techniques including liquid nitrogen (LN2), carbon Quantum dots with SQL (CD), and oil with small quantity lubrication (OSQL) produce larger ferrite grains, which lower hardness. According to residual stress analysis, the high temperatures during dry cutting result in increased strains, which are successfully mitigated by lubrication. The two-phase microstructure has a crucial influence in mechanical characteristics, as demonstrated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies, wherein finer grains improve surface polish but demand more energy. Measuring surface roughness (Ra) reveals that adhesion wears during dry machining raises Ra, while OSQL and LN2 cooling lower Ra and improve surface quality. Tool performance is negatively impacted by high machining temperatures. Cutting temperatures and friction are greatly reduced by LN2 cooling. Dry machining increases tool wear because of the high temperatures and absence of lubrication; OSQL and CD lower wear rates, while LN2 offers the best protection for tools. Thinner, better-separated chips are produced by effective lubrication in OSQL and LN2, while thicker, more deformed chips are produced by dry machining, according to chip morphology and thickness analyses. The study leads to the conclusion that super duplex steel's surface quality, tool life, and machinability can all be significantly enhanced by carbon quantum dots and cryogenic cooling.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The objective of this research is to examine how various cooling and lubrication techniques affect the machinability of Duplex F53 steel when turning it, with particular attention to residual stress, surface roughness, chip morphology, tool wear, machining temperature, and crystallographic structures. According to the microstructure analysis, the presence of additional austenite grains at high temperatures during dry machining results in an increase in hardness. On the other hand, cooling techniques including liquid nitrogen (LN2), carbon Quantum dots with SQL (CD), and oil with small quantity lubrication (OSQL) produce larger ferrite grains, which lower hardness. According to residual stress analysis, the high temperatures during dry cutting result in increased strains, which are successfully mitigated by lubrication. The two-phase microstructure has a crucial influence in mechanical characteristics, as demonstrated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies, wherein finer grains improve surface polish but demand more energy. Measuring surface roughness (Ra) reveals that adhesion wears during dry machining raises Ra, while OSQL and LN2 cooling lower Ra and improve surface quality. Tool performance is negatively impacted by high machining temperatures. Cutting temperatures and friction are greatly reduced by LN2 cooling. Dry machining increases tool wear because of the high temperatures and absence of lubrication; OSQL and CD lower wear rates, while LN2 offers the best protection for tools. Thinner, better-separated chips are produced by effective lubrication in OSQL and LN2, while thicker, more deformed chips are produced by dry machining, according to chip morphology and thickness analyses. The study leads to the conclusion that super duplex steel's surface quality, tool life, and machinability can all be significantly enhanced by carbon quantum dots and cryogenic cooling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Duplex steels (DSS) are the combination of austenitic and ferritic based alloys [1] which are widely preferred in nuclear, marine and oil industries [2]. Because ferrite is included in DSS, the material's mechanical strength has increased, making it harder [3]. Poor machinability led to high tensile stress, which in turn shortened the machined part's lifespan. Additionally, a rise in temperature was noted in the cutting area, which contributes to tool wear. The main problem mentioned in DSS is the development of built-up layers [4]. In addition, other issues with machining DSS include incorrect cutting-edge growth and inadequate heat dissipation capability [5]. Therefore, it is really necessary to select a good lubricant that can solve the aforementioned issues. When Gupta et al. [6] examined the effect of the cryogenic lubricant on the turning of Ti-6Al-4V alloy, they found that, under N2+MQL, surface roughness was reduced at all combinations of feed rate and cutting speed, and that, as a result of improved lubrication, the surface was further exposed as smooth in a 3D surface topography study. By using hybrid cryogenic cooling, the specific cutting energy is decreased while the surface area's microhardness is raised. Chauhan et al. [7] studied the machining performance of Ti-6Al-4V under dry and cryogenic techniques and observed machining time increased by 83 % and 39 % at lower spindle speeds (80 and 90 m/min) compared to 100 m/min in a cryogenic environment, leading to improved surface finish. While dry machining consumed approximately 5 %, 26 %, and 27 % minimum power in comparison with cryogenic conditions at 80, 90, and 100 m/min spindle speeds, correspondingly. Demirsoz et al. [8] examined the tribological characteristics of 100Cr6 alloy and additively manufactured 316 L stainless steel under various cooling scenarios. They found that, in contrast to dry, MQL, and cryogenic cooling, cryo-MQL lubrication produced the lowest volume losses (0.1040 mm³) and wear depth values (20 μm). In their investigation into the impacts of TiN-coated cutting inserts on AISI D3 steel alloy, Sharma et al. [9] found that the best conditions are achieved with LN2 machining at the following parameters: temperature (25.40 °C), cutting force (28.01 N), flank wear length (61.97 μm), and surface roughness (0.75 μm). Liquid nitrogen is regarded as a cooler that is better for the environment. Wang et al. [10] used a LN2-compressed air combination to study hardened stainless steel FV520B. The reduction of cutting force, vibration, and surface roughness was seen with low-temperature cooling. At −100 °C, notable improvements in machinability were seen, especially with increased cutting rates, feed per tooth, and axial cutting depth. Researchers Gupta et al. [11] looked at how various cooling schedules affected the machining of titanium alloys. Hybrid LN2 + MQL reduced cutting temperature and friction while increasing tool life and surface quality. Specific cutting energy (SCE) and cutting temperature were affected by cooling circumstances; the hybrid LN2 + MQL displayed the lowest SCE.

The machinability and stress corrosion cracking (SCC) behaviour of super duplex DSS was examined by Rajaguru and Arunachalam [12]. All coolant conditions have an impact on DSS machining due to thermal effects. There was evidence of residual tensile stress and susceptibility to corrosion aided by the environment. In comparison to dry cutting, MQL and flood conditions decreased tensile residual stress, which resulted in a lower stress corrosion fracture density. Gupta et al. [13] analysed the tribological properties in turning of duplex SS and confirmed that the energy consumption was low with 203.67 kJ under MQL3. Further, the minimum quantity lubrication exhibits more ferrite grains due to fast cooling. The SEM analysis indicates minimal BUE formation because of adhesion and abrasion marks. In order to resolve ranking conflicts among Multi-Attribute Decision Making approaches, Koyee et al. [14] evaluated the machining of stainless-steel grades (EN 1.4404, EN 1.4462, and EN 1.4410) under constant cutting speed using the fuzzy approach. The main factor influencing the final surface quality of stainless steel was found to be the feed rate. For every material, the ideal factor level combinations were found, leading to notable enhancements in surface quality. de Oliveira Junior et al. [15] has done a turning operation on SAF 2507 duplex SS and confirmed that the high-pressure cooling system has improved the tool life and reduced the roughness of the workpiece. Binali et al. [16] found that, MQL and dry machining are used to assess the turning conditions of AISI 304. Important discoveries show that MQL greatly lowers cutting forces and temperatures, improving machining performance overall. greater feed rates typically result in greater cutting temperatures and rougher surfaces; cutting speed is shown to be the most noteworthy factor prompting surface roughness. Furthermore, chip morphology is influenced by the type of lubricant, and chip morphology is influenced by machining quality. The findings show that MQL enhances machinability, hence promoting more environmentally friendly machining procedures for AISI 304 stainless steel.

Krolczyk and Legutko [17] performed experimental study to determine surface roughness in Duplex Stainless Steel (DSS) dry machining following turning with coated sintered carbide wedges. It was discovered that a tiny initial contact area is possible with dry cutting, which lowers friction for lubricated surfaces. The investigation was carried out in a deep-well pump and electric motor component manufacturing facility. Feed rate, cutting speed, usage of a cooling and lubricating liquid based on mineral oil, and roughness profile were the primary factors affecting surface roughness. Dry cutting raised the R
k parameter, which resulted in debris entrapment and lubricant retention. Significant cost reductions are possible due to the established surface roughness prediction model's ability to anticipate surface roughness. Krolczyk et al. [18] presented the approach for streamlining the manufacture of hard-to-cut materials, especially DSS. Analyzing turning experimental data with the Taguchi method with orthogonal array L9 and signal-to-noise ratio is done through the Design of Experiment (DOE). According to the study, tool durability is impacted by feed rate and cutting speed by 20 % and 60 %, respectively. 50.27 min and 40.78 min are estimated to be the ideal cutting conditions. In their work Ross et al. [19] the effects of four environmental regimes (flood, dry, MQL, and N-MQL) on AMed 316L stainless steel specimen metal cutting is examined. The effectiveness of N-MQL lubrication using rice bran oil and carbon dots (CDs) particles is the main focus of the work. According to the results, N-MQL works better than other cutting regimes and provides better surface quality and machining performance. According to the study, N-MQL is an essential option for AMed specimens since it lowers cutting temperature, surface roughness (Ra), tool wear, microstructure, and micro-hardness. Using the N-MQL cutting method, the 3D surface topography analysis showed a smooth surface with fewer lows and peaks. The higher level of C/L, which is represented in the refinement of grain size, indicates a direct relationship between the performance of N-MQL and the hardness on the machined side. Leksycki et al. [20] inspected the surface topography of 17-4 PH stainless steel under wet, dry, and near-dry cutting environments. Using an optical profilometer, the study employed the “parameter space investigation” method to assess material participation curves, contour maps, and surface roughness characteristics. In comparison to dry machining, the study indicated that near-dry circumstances reduced the Sa, Sq, and Sz parameters by 38–48 %. For lower feeds and average cutting speeds, valleys and ridges were noted; for higher feeds and intermediate cutting speeds, deep valleys and high ridges were noted. The study additionally found that the surface texture had a mixed anisotropic character under all inspected cooling conditions, with deep pits and high peaks typical for turning feed effect and irregular tiny feed traces. Krolczyk et al. [21] studied the impact of green production techniques on the surface morphology of DSS. Power Spectral Density is used to compare the surface quality between MQCL and dry turning techniques. Higher wavelengths for dry turning than MQCL techniques are shown in the results. At lower feed rates, tool vibration and edge wear take center stage, whereas the feed component's amplitude becomes apparent at higher feed ranges. The paper is pertinent to surface metrology specialists and presents a fresh method for surface quality analysis. In their study, Krolczyk et al. [22] analysed the wear mechanisms that occur during DSS turning on the major flank and rake face of coated carbide tool points. The rake face displayed adhesive interaction, flank wear, and abrasive wear, according to the results. For 3D surface measuring, the Infinite Focus optical equipment and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) were employed. The study found that flank wear is the most common failure mode, wear severity increases with increasing cutting speed, and the suggested approach reliably detects wear on the wedge's rake face. The longevity and surface topography of coated carbide tools used in duplex stainless steel turning are examined by Krolczyk et al. [23]. It identifies challenges such strong adhesive interaction, excessive mechanical and thermal stresses, and chip control. Additionally, the study shows that CVD-Ti(C, N)/Al2O3/TiN coated carbide tools exhibit greater resistance against abrasive wear and are suggested for DSS roughing machining. Additionally, the study discovered that the metallographic structure during DSS turning was unaffected by cooling or cutting speed.

Further, the increase in fluid pressure increases the corrosion resistance, which is necessary to reduce tool wear. Electrospinning was used by Nie et al. [24] to create the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in polyacrylonitrile nanofibers. The mechanical characteristics of the nanofiber structure were unaffected by the uniform distribution of CQDs. Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria were subjected to antibacterial photodynamic inactivation using PAN-CQD nanofibers. Potential for anti-infection applications was demonstrated by the excellent antibacterial activity reported against E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and B. subtilis. Water-soluble CQDs and their additions in water-based lubricants for Si3N4-steel and Si3N4-Si3N4 contacts were analysed by Xiao et al. [25] For Si3N4-steel and Si3N4-Si3N4 contacts, the maximum reduction in coefficient of friction (COF) was 14 % and 30 %, respectively. The spherical form and graphene-layered structure of CQDs are responsible for the lubricating effect, which lowers resistance forces during relative motion. Krolczyk et al. [26] examined the efficient machining strategies for duplex stainless steel, aiming to reduce environmental and health impacts from cutting fluids and lower energy use. Through turning tests with carbide tools, under dry and lubricated conditions, they found that dry turning, with optimal tool selection and parameters, significantly increases tool life by three times compared to lubricated cutting. This enhancement is attributed to better management of chip control and reduced stress on tools. Narayanan and Jagadeesha [27] analysed the effect of cryogenic cooling on turning super duplex steel 2507 using tungsten carbide inserts and found that a cryogenically treated tool with direct LN2 significantly increased tool life by 23 % compared to a cryogenically treated hardened tool. Narayanan et al. [28] performed experiments in turning of duplex SS and noted a significant improvement in tool life, with a 23 % increase for direct LN2 supply over cryogenically tempered tools. The study also exhibited an 8 % improvement in insert hardness through tempering, contributing to prolonged tool life. Dhananchezian et al. [29] studied the temperature and surface roughness when turning Duplex Stainless Steel 2205 and confirmed a 53–58 % reduction in cutting temperature and 18–23 % decrease in roughness compared to dry turning. Kanagaraju et al. [30] examined the ecological drilling of SDSS 2507 using cryogenic machining and biodegradable MQL and found that cryogenic condition reduced cutting temperature, improved surface finish, and improved chip breaking compared to other methods. Sivaiah and Chakradhar [31] performed experimental study in turning 17-4 PH stainless steel using a new cryogenic cooling method. An innovative jet tool holder that simultaneously delivers liquid nitrogen to the rake and side surfaces significantly reduced cutting temperature and tool wear by 61 % and 29 % respectively. Sivaiah and Chakradhar [32] studied the machining of 17-4 PH stainless steel and proved that their low-cost external spray cooling cryogenic setup efficiently utilised liquid nitrogen, yielding superior results in cutting temperature reduction, minimal tool wear and improved surface integrity. Sivaiah et al. [33] studied the need for environmentally friendly practices in improving machining parameters and confirmed that MQL cooling with textured tools significantly reduced cutting temperature by up to 13 %, flank wear by 17 %, and surface roughness by 29 % compared to non-textured tools. Sivaiah et al. [34] examined the effectiveness of hybrid textured tools (HTT) in turning 17-4 PH steel and showed that the HTT significantly reduced cutting zone temperature by 43 %, tool wear by 42 %, and surface roughness by 45 % compared to dry cutting. A series of studies were carried out [35–38], to evaluate machinability and sustainability using dry, oil, carbon quantum dots, and LN2 by analysing surface roughness, machining temperature, tool wear, and chip characteristics. The research studies indicated that cryogenic cooling and carbon quantum dots improved machining efficiency and reduce environmental impact. However, studies on their suitability to achieve good surface finish in duplex steel machining and their comparative sustainability were limited.

Pimenov et al. [39] observed that dry machining reduces environmental impact and increases efficiency, making it a sustainable production method. More research is necessary to fully understand how cooling and lubrication affect tool performance in this situation. Minimising environmental harm requires effective waste management, which includes recycling and repurposing liquid and solid waste like chips and cutting equipment. Pimenov et al. [40] noticed that effective lubrication and cooling methods are critical to contemporary machining. Although hybrid cryogenic technologies are even more effective, MQL performs better than dry machining by minimising tool wear and improving surface roughness. When it comes to reducing cutting forces and wear, coated tools outperform uncoated ones. Low-porosity super alloys are excellently produced by additive manufacturing; however, their hardness makes machining them more difficult. While reducing the risks to the environment and human health, dry machining still requires proper lubrication in order to maximize tool life while milling super alloys. This work examines these problems in order to optimize machining procedures for advanced materials. Machado et al. [41] found how important machinability indices, such as material removal rate (MRR), chip formation, tool wear, and tool life, relate to the mechanical properties of steel. It looks at ways to improve machinability by using cutting-edge cooling methods including MQL, Cryogenic Cooling, and High-Pressure Cooling in conjunction with dry cutting. Assessments are also conducted on the effects of new materials and coatings, as well as tool design, on machining performance. Further investigation is conducted into the possibility of auxiliary machining methods, such as vibrational and thermal approaches, and other operations like drilling, turning, milling, and grinding, to enhance surface integrity and overall machinability. Furthermore, the energy consumption and carbon emissions resulting from machining operations are substantial, underscoring the necessity of optimization measures. This work emphasizes to attain better sustainability in manufacturing, by lowering production costs, creating environmentally friendly machining procedures, and comprehending how tool geometry affects energy usage.

It is evident from the earlier research that cryogenic chilling is an ideal method for enhancing the machinability of different alloys. Also, it is observed from literature results cryogenic conditions pave way to sustainable coolant in machining process. Research papers were published in analysing the machinability of steel only under cryogenic conditions. The research gaps identified were (i) Work related in turning duplex steel when LN2 and carbon quantum dots as the lubricant were found to be rare, (ii) Comparison of LN2 and CD from the sustainability point of view of “Go-Green” was not explored. Thus, the following objectives identified are: (a) R
a analysis using DM, OSQL, CD, and LN2 (b) Machining temperature and tool wear on DM, OSQL, CD, and LN2 (c) Thoroughly analysing the shape and thickness of the chips in duplex steel and (iv) To evaluate the relative sustainability among various environmental conditions. The work's novelty lies in the synthesis of quantum dots, which were used as lubricant relatively with oil and LN2. In this regard, tests were conducted in turning duplex steel for R
a
, M
t
, TW, and C
t under conditions of dry, oil, carbon quantum dots, and LN2. Surface roughness, machining temperature, tool wear, chip thickness, and chip morphology are used to measure machinability performance of duplex steels.

The microstructure of duplex steel revel austenite phase (γ) and ferrite phase (α). A cylindrical duplex steel with a diameter of 30 mm and a length of 1100 mm, known for its excellent resistance to crevice corrosion, is chosen for the experimental work. This type of steel is commonly employed in many applications such as propeller shafts, pressure containers, piston rods, and chemical industries. The chemical composition, expressed in weight percent (wt%), includes the following elements: C = 0.03 %, Cr = 24.23 %, Mn = 0.93 %, Mo = 3.88 %, N = 0.28 %, Ni = 6.87 %, P = 0.022 %, Si = 2.04 %, S = 0.02 % and Fe = remaining. The turning insert utilised was a TNMG 160408 MT triangle-shaped tool manufactured by Taegutec. It was coated using PVD (Physical Vapour Deposition) using a combination of TiAlN and TiN. The inserts had a corner radius of 0.8 mm and a rear rake angle of 5°. The samples were machined for a length of 30 mm in three consecutive trials during the experiments. The particulars for conducting experiments are outlined in Table 1 . The values were nominated based on the previous research work. The reason is that cutting speed and feed are the most significant variables in affecting the measured responses [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots and SQL system
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesised using carbonisation, a process that involves the decomposition of an organic molecule into residue using pyrolysis. A 25 g sample of citric acid was heated in a round-bottomed flask, increasing the temperature from 170 to 200 °C over a period of 30 min [43]. During the combustion process, citric acid undergoes a slow transition from a colourless state to a light-yellow state, and ultimately to a yellowish orange state. Following the chilling process, a high-viscosity liquid with an orange coloration was produced. Subsequently, the liquid was agitated with 50 ml of water that had undergone double distillation. The acidic solution was brought to a pH of 7 by gradually adding 10 ml of a 1N NaOH solution drop by drop, while maintaining a constant temperature [44]. A highly concentrated colloidal fluid consisting of a significant amount of brownish black CD powder was created [45].

The SQL system (Fig. 1 ) produced by KENCO (Krishna Engg Company- INDIA) was used to deliver the oil and carbon quantum dots to the machining portion. It comprises of an air compressor that raises the air pressure and enables it to enter the mixing chamber. The cutting fluid (Accu Lube LB-2000 and carbon quantum dots) in the mixing chamber combine with air, resulting in the formation of a mist that is then directed to the cutting area. The manufacturer provides the following standard specifications for Accu-Lube LB 2000: (i) appearance: blue, (ii) density (g/cm3): 0.92, and (iii) flash point (°C): 320. To deliver LN2, the same SQL system is not preferred. The boiling point of nitrogen is −196 °C, so it cannot be used in liquid form in ambient conditions. Thus, a tube that was vacuum sealed was preferred to supply LN2 from a tank where it is stored at a pressure of 500 kPa. The values regarding flow rate, pressure and diameter of nozzle are listed in Table 1.

The Surfcorder SE 3500, equipped with a 4 mm evaluation length and a resolution of 0.1 nm, is employed for recording the two-dimensional R
a value. In order to mitigate any discrepancies in the data, the R
a measurements were conducted thrice at three distinct sites on the samples. A non-contact technology, namely the noncontact Talysurf CCI Lite, was utilised to do 3D measurements of the surface roughness and microstructure of the machined materials. The FLIR C5 thermal camera with image frequency = 8.7 Hz, thermal sensitivity of <70 mK, spectral range = 8–14 μm, range of temperature = −20 °C–400 °C and accuracy of ±3 % is used to assess the temperature at the machining site using thermographic images. The device was positioned at a distance of 1m from the machining zone as a precautionary measure to protect it from any damage caused by lubrication or chip dispersion. The examination of flank wear, crater wear, and chip thickness was conducted using a Thermoscientific-Apreo S field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) with a beam current of up to 50 nA. The progression of tool wear (namely, flank wear) was quantified using the VMS-2010F video measuring system, which allowed for magnification ranging from 30× to 190x with a resolution of 0.5 μm. The zeta potential was determined using the Nano zeta potential analyzer ZS-90 equipment. The SEM (Hitachi S 3400N) was employed to record the carbon quantum dots. The structure of quantum dots was observed using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) with a JOEL JEM-2100 Plus instrument from Japan. The UV–Visible spectrometer (Shimadzu -UV 3600 plus, USA) was used to record the absorption spectra within the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The pH values were determined using a pH metre (Global: DPH 500, India). The angle of contact of the machining fluids was analysed using a drop analyzer (Kruss GMBH, DSA25B) at room temperature. The Fourier Transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) was used to observe transmittance in the range of 4000 to 500 cm-1 using a Shimadzu- IRAffinity-1 instrument, with a maximum resolution of 0.5 cm−1. Before the test, a small number of samples were placed on the FTIR table for evaluation. The X-ray diffractometer chosen for this study is the Bruker D8 Advance Davinci. It is used to determine the distinct phases of the materials under different cooling conditions. The diffraction measurements are taken between the range of 10°–80° (2θ). The Devices preferred for the study are depicted in Fig. 2 .

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the carbon quantum dots (CD) are depicted in Fig. 3 a. TEM images (Fig. 3b) reveal a restricted distribution of CD, arranged in the form of spherical spots [46]. The crystallographic organisation of the CD was analysed using the technique of Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED). When a beam of electrons strikes the samples, it results in the formation of a diffraction pattern [47]. Here, the scanning electron diffraction (SAED) analysis of the CD structure (Fig. 3c) revealed a pattern resembling rings [48]. Moreover, distinct lattice boundaries of CD may be readily recognised, indicating the substantial presence of graphite in the form of carbon. The presence of CD is clearly indicated by the Letter “C" (Fig. 3d) seen in the EDS analysis.

Fig. 4 shows the UV–Vis plot of CD in the wave length from 200 to 800 nm. It lies on the principle of Beer-Lamber's which propose absorbance is directly proportional to the particle concentration. The CD reveal absorbance spectra at 236 nm (point 4) because of π→π∗ change of aromatic sp2 region (C=C). Moreover, a maximum absorption also noticed at 374 nm (point 3) due to n→π∗ alteration of sp3 mixed area (C=O) [49].

The FTIR spectra (Fig. 5 ) of carbon quantum dots were analysed to identify the presence of certain chemical groups in the sample. The FTIR analysis of CD shows absorption peaks resulting from the presence of O-H, C=C, C-N, C-OH, and C-O-C functional groups. The highest peak seen in the range of 3250 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of O-H functional groups (hydroxyl) that were manually absorbed on a CD [50]. Furthermore, the observed absorption bands at 1710, 1554, 1379, and 1271 cm−1 exhibit a resemblance to certain functional groups known as carboxyl (C=O). Furthermore, epoxy compounds such as C=C, C-N, C-OH, and C-O-C are accessible [51,52]. The presence of functional constituents in CD plays a crucial role in determining its solubility in an aqueous solution [53].

The photostability of CD may be determined by subjecting it to UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm. The CD has a pale-yellow colour (Fig. 6 a) when seen under normal lighting conditions [54]. Furthermore, when viewed with UV light (Fig. 6b) at a wavelength of 365 nm, the substance exhibits a blue hue [54], indicating the presence of carbon quantum dots in an aqueous solution.

Zeta potential (ZP) refers to the electric potential at the shear plane of a colloid particle that changes according to the action of electric force. The ZP of the collides varies from ±30 to ±40 mV, which indicates a high level of consistency [55]. The carbon quantum dots that were synthesised exhibited a zeta potential of −32mV (Fig. 7 ), indicating that the produced CD is stable in its current state [48].

To achieve stability for samples over an extended period, it is necessary to separate pH values from the isoelectric point. The pH values of the samples to be evaluated are modified using acidic (HCL) and alkaline (NaOH) solutions. To provide optimal stability for CD, the pH levels should range from 5 to 11 [56]. The pH of the synthesised carbon quantum dots (Fig. 8 ) was measured to be 6.97, suggesting that the generated sample was stable.

The contact angle is the crucial metric for assessing the cooling performance in tribological behaviour. The acculube oil exhibits a wettability angle (θa) of 42.7° and a carbon quantum dots (θc) of 19°, as seen in Fig. 9 . However, it has been shown that a greater angle of contact leads to a diminished cooling effect of the coolant [57]. θc is found to be less than θa, indicating that CD is a more effective coolant than acculube oil for machining operations.

Fig. 10 illustrates the microstructure achieved while spinning duplex steel under various circumstances. Phase change in duplex steel happens due to plastic deformation [58]. Microstructure analysis provides a clear understanding of the material's machinability. Austenite (white) and ferrite (black) are observable in duplex steel [57]. In dry mode (Fig. 10a), the hardness of the material increases due to that ferrite structure which are ductile and soft in nature are observed very less [59]. However, a significant presence of austenite grains was seen due to the fact that austenite is a kind of steel grains that form at high temperatures and are often observed in dry conditions [59]. Moreover, it is evident that the size of the ferrite grains significantly increases during cutting processes performed under OSQL, CD, and LN2 modes as listed in Fig. 10 b,c,d. This is attributed to the work material's temperature under the surface surpassing the austenite temperature as a result of the cooling mechanism. Additionally, it was observed that MQL results in larger grains compared to the dry mode, primarily due to an increase in ferrite grain. Consequently, a decrease in hardness was observed when using MQL [59]. However, the elevated heat at the cutting tool leads to thermal softening in the machined samples, which needs to be minimised [59].

Residual stress refers to the stress that remains in the work material even after the tool and work sample are no longer in contact. The stresses were created in two directions, namely circumferential and axial, in the machined portion. The act of measuring in the direction of the feed lines indicates the presence of circumferential tension, whereas measuring perpendicular to the feed lines on the work component indicates the presence of axial stress. The residual stress is solely determined by the temperature reached in the machining zone. The stress values obtained for two directions at maximum feed (0.2 mm/rev) under various cutting circumstances (Fig. 11 ) disclose that the dry cutting method resulted in increased stress due to elevated temperatures at the cutting contact [12]. Nevertheless, lubrication applied during OSQL, CD, and LN2 machining resulted in a decrease in residual stress to a certain degree at the cutting location. Moreover, a significant amount of stress was encountered in a circular manner in relation to the axial direction [12].

Fig. 12 Illustrates the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analytical results for different machining settings applied in turning operations on F-53 steel. The observed peaks corresponding to the ferrite and austenite phases on the machined surfaces highlight the two-phase microstructure of the material, which is a critical balance between strength and durability, ensuring excellent mechanical properties and corrosion resistance. These peaks tell us about the phase composition and distribution of the steel, which is crucial in tailoring the machining process to maintain material integrity and efficiency in demanding applications. The grain size of Duplex F53 steel greatly affects its machinability. Finer grains lead to rise in tool wear and a better surface finish but require more energy for machining due to the material's higher hardness and strength. On the other hand, coarser grains can reduce tool wear and energy consumption but can reduce surface finish. In addition, grain size affects chip formation, with finer grains helping to make chips smoother. Optimizing machining parameters and lubrication methods for Duplex F53 steel requires careful consideration of these factors and the selection of appropriate tools and strategies to balance tool life, surface quality, and machining efficiency. The higher dislocation density in F53 duplex steel increases hardness and strength, resulting in more complex machining, increased tool wear, and optimized machining and lubrication strategies. In general, the precipitation of intermetallic compounds makes duplex stainless-steel brittle. The possible presence of compounds between metals, e.g. sigma and chi, of duplex steel F53 significantly affects its machinability and mechanical properties [60]. Harder and more brittle than the austenitic-ferritic matrix of steel, these compounds increase tool wear, requiring more durable machining strategies and more efficient lubrication methods [60]. The absence of these intermetallic compounds generally improves machinability. With a controlled cooling rate under non-isothermal conditions resulting in less than 1 % sigma and chi phase volume. The sigma (σ) and chi (χ) phases of duplex stainless steel can be detected by XRD, as a lower percentage may have caused the XRD tool to miss the peak detection [60].

Before the test, the samples underwent wirecut EDM machining. The plots displayed distinct peaks corresponding to atomic planes characteristic of ferrite and austenite phases. Furthermore, there were no indications of intermetallic compounds present in the work material throughout any of the test settings. The breadth of the peak is contingent upon the enhancement in the grains as well as the substantial dislocation density. No substantial phase transition takes place on the surface of the work material under dry, OSQL, and CD conditions. The presence of austenite phase (γ) and ferrite phase (α) observed at an angle between 42° and 75° [61]. Machining samples in a cryogenic environment leads to an increase in peak size, indicating a noticeable improvement in grain quality on the machined surfaces.

The surface topography is of paramount importance in achieving high precision in the turning process. Typically, the Ra value should be kept as low as possible to provide optimal efficiency in the machining process. The choice of coolant or lubrication is one of the factors that influences the roughness of the machined component. Fig. 13 a displays the 2-dimensional profilometries of surfaces acquired in dry conditions. Here, a high R
a is seen because to the wide temperature range and the stresses created near the edge of the inserts [62]. The presence of significant adhesion wear was observed due to intense abrasion occurring between the flank and crater surfaces of the inserts. Consequently, the chips exhibited extensive serration and experienced substantial plastic deformation, resulting in the production of low-quality machined surfaces. The R
a values obtained were significantly lowered while using OSQL because to the reduction in abrasion caused by reducing the temperature in the machining area [63]. The occurrence of built-up edges was significantly reduced as a result of adequate lubrication given by Minimum Quantity Lubrication (MQL) in the tool-work regions, hence enhancing the surface quality. Nevertheless, the presence of a protective coating applied to the cutting area also plays a crucial role in improving the quality of the machined surfaces. When comparing surfaces formed with CD, the R
a value was lower than surfaces generated with DM and OSQL conditions. The spherical configuration of carbon quantum dots induces a rolling motion, resulting in a decrease in the coefficient of friction (COF) [25]. The reduction in COF indicates optimal lubrication at the machining location. Moreover, the reparative properties of quantum dots aid in filling the void generated on the machined surface, resulting in a decrease in roughness measurements. According to Fig. 13a It is evident that the use of cryogenic coolant (LN2) has significantly reduced roughness compared to other cooling methods. The length of the chip-tool contact and the frictional force were reduced under cryogenic conditions. By distributing cryogenic coolant to the machining region, the stiffness of the machined samples is enhanced, resulting in reduced coefficients of friction (COF). In addition, liquid nitrogen (LN2) efficiently removes heat from the cutting area and provides enough cooling, resulting in a smooth texture [11]. As liquid nitrogen evaporates, it expands rapidly into nitrogen gas, which can create additional pressure that might assist in removing chips and debris from the cutting area. The direct benefit in machining surface quality from the rapid expansion of evaporated liquid nitrogen is that the created pressure helps remove chips and debris more effectively, reducing their influence on the surface finish and leading to a smoother, more precise machining finish. Examining the three-dimensional profile of the surface is crucial as it directly impacts the fatigue life of the material. The 3D structure (Fig. 14) undergoes significant alterations under dry conditions, exhibiting prominent peaks and deep troughs as a result of increased abrasion. The occurrence of deep valleys and sudden peaks in OSQL was mitigated to some extent by the use of lubricant at the contact between the tool and the workpiece. However, the CD method resulted in a reduction of the immediate trough and deep troughs compared to the OSQL and DM methods. The surface exhibits improved appearance when treated with liquid nitrogen, since it eliminates prominent peaks and deepens the valleys. The red hue in the photos represents a coarse texture, while the green hue indicates a moderate finish, and the blue hue signifies a smooth finish on the machined component. Broad friction tracks (Fig. 13 b) were noticed with dry conditions because of absence of lubrication. However, there is a slight reduction in friction tracks found with OSQL (Fig. 13b) and CD (Fig. 13b) machining conditions due to sufficient defensive layer provided at the surface during turning process. Machining with LN2 provided a drastic decrease in friction which paved way to minimise the development of friction tracks as noticed in Fig. 13b.

In order to achieve sliding, the parameter known as Sp-maximum peak height was utilised extensively; hence, it is being considered. During this time, Sv was explored because the maximum valley depth is linked to the retention of lubrication on the surfaces that were exposed to machining. The values that were attained while using DM in the Sv parameter (Fig. 14 ) were 32.31 μm. The absence of coolant is the cause of these values being created. On the other hand, the sufficient lubrication that was produced under OSQL and CD conditions led to an increase in the Sv parameter by 34.53 μm and 36 μm, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 14. In comparison to DM, the Sv parameter increased by 12.4 % when the machining process was carried out under cryogenic conditions. One of the lowest values of Sz, which is the sum of Sp and Sv , was 47.17 μm, which was achieved by the use of DM. However, it was noted that there was a considerable increase in trend with OSQL measuring 51.78 μm, CD measuring 51.94 μm, and LN2 measuring 53.23 μm. Fig. 14 illustrates the contour maps, which show the peaks and valleys that are denoted by the dotted lines. The height parameter Ssk (skewness) explains the machined surface co-ordinates distribution [64]. Negative values for Ssk are typically indicative of the presence of deep valleys in the surfaces that have been machined. In the case of all DM, OSQL, CD, and LN2 circumstances, it was noted that the Ssk values were less than zero, indicating a definite decrease in the number of peaks compared to the number of valleys. This was observed in Fig. 14. The measure known as kurtosis (Sku) reveals the different types of peaks that are present on the machined sample. In the event where the Sku value (Fig. 14) is less than 3, the peak profile appears to be flattened; hence, it is classed as platykurtic [65]. On the other hand, when Sku is greater than 3, the peak contour has a tendency to be conical in shape (Fig. 14), which is a characteristic that falls within the category of leptokurtic characteristics. Machining under dry conditions results in a high Sku of 3.24 (Fig. 14), which is a leptokurtic [65]. The lowest Sku observed with OSQL and LN2 is 3, which means that the profile of these surfaces displays a strident peak profile. These profiles will be erased during the decohesion process. Additionally, as compared to other machining circumstances, the kurtosis is noted to be significant when the material is in a dry condition (Fig. 14). This is a strong signal that there are flaws on the surfaces [66]. As per the observations, it was discovered that the root mean square height (Sq) reached its highest point when the DM condition (Fig. 14) was 9.7 μm. The reason for this is that the lack of lubricant causes friction, which in turn causes an increase in the roughness of the machined item. In contrast, the smallest squares formed by LN2 (Fig. 14) were 9 μm. Because of the effective shielding at the cutting zone, the amount of heat produced is reduced, which in turn reduces the roughness value. The most important component in improving the surface quality is the enhancement of the lubrication at the tool-workpiece region [67] With LN2, a reduction of 7.2 % in Sq was recorded over dry conditions.

The influence of cutting speed (Vc) and feed rate (f) on the surface roughness (R
a
) is illustrated in Fig. 15 . Here, a decrease in Ra is observed as an increase in Vc for all cutting conditions. As Vc is increased, a significant amount of heat is released, which produce thermal relaxation of the processed samples. Thus, a significant reduction in Ra was achieved by removing a significant number of rough and damaged surfaces [59]. Additionally, an increase in temperature with increasing Vc was observed because it removes the built-in edge (BUE), resulting in a significant decrease in roughness values [68]. The increase in feed rate leads to a more pronounced intensification of Ra values, since the higher feed rate amplifies the rubbing action in the tool-work part area, hence resulting in an increase in R
a .

The crucial factors evaluated in terms of tool life are the commercial, environmental, and manufacturing costs. Moreover, the value of the machined product is solely determined by the extent of tool wear and the type of lubrication selected. The lifespan of the tool was determined by the extent of flank, crater, and corner wear [73]. The present study examines the processes of flank wear and crater wear in duplex steel during turning operations conducted under different machining conditions. Cutting under dry circumstances (Fig. 18 a) leads to rapid wear of the cutting edges due to the high temperatures generated [71]. The irregular wear surface facilitates easier attachment at the tool-chip zone, resulting in the production of BUE. Abrasion wear is a significant issue in dry conditions, resulting in extensive tool wear. The abrasion wear (Fig. 18a) occurred as a result of tool breakage, with chips flowing over the flank region and crater region. In addition, the attachment of chips (Fig. 18a) and the application of severe tension on the tool result in increased friction, leading to abrasion wear. The presence of chip pieces on the insert face can be explained by the chemical affinity between the work material and the tool material, as seen in Fig. 18b. The EDS analysis and mapping (Fig. 18c) reveal a significant presence of iron (Fe) in the workpiece material adhered to the insert, suggesting the extent of dry machining.

The accumulation of chips in the rake zone (Fig. 19 a) raises the temperature as the chips are not being removed owing to the lack of cutting fluid in a dry environment. Consequently, an increase in friction is seen, causing the chips to adhere to the surface of the rake and leading to the formation of crater wear under dry conditions. Additionally, significant stress causes work samples to adhere to the surface of the crater, leading to the formation of BUE [74]. Nevertheless, these BUE formations are transient and are removed by the chips produced during the machining process. During the process of relocation, a small number of tools (insert samples) were taken out. Therefore, the insert is susceptible to experiencing wear and tear. Small cases of adhesion and detachment of the covering layer were observed (Fig. 19a), as well as the remains of the working material (chips). In the dry machining, pieces of the workpiece adhere to the surface of the tool, and as processing continues, this opens the way for the coating on the tool to loosen, leading to removal of coating. The theory of work material debris trapped in the crater region is due to the lack of inertia in the chips, developed to reduce friction in the tool-chip region [59]. In general, bonding of BUE formations with carbide parts was common. In addition, high heat was obtained during processing with duplex steel, so BUE and adhesive wear were often observed during dry condition [13]. Additionally, adhesive wear results in the formation of a built-up layer (BUL). Upon EDS with mapping micrographs (Fig. 19b and c), the presence of Fe, Mo, and Cr elements on the insert rake face confirms the occurrence of workpiece material adhering to the insert face.

OSQL machining significantly decreased the production of BUE compared to DM. OSQL machining significantly reduces built-up edge (BUE) formation by providing a protective layer that lowers friction and enhances heat dissipation [75]. In addition, an effective heat dissipation method was implemented by the evaporation of droplets. Chipping and abrasion (Fig. 20 a) were seen during OSQL, however, there was a little decrease in comparison to the dry mode. The SEM images and EDS analysis (Fig. 20b and c) reveal reduced iron (Fe) adhesion on the tool, with a decrease from 83 % in dry machining to 69 % in OSQL. While chipping and crater wear persist, the extent is less severe than in dry conditions, and the presence of elements like Mo, Cr, and Ni on the tool's surface is relatively minor.

Upon examination of the crater surface, the presence of BUL and chipping was predominantly evident, as seen in the accompanying Fig. 21 a. However, a little decrease in contrast was noticed as compared to the dry state. The acculube oil effectively lubricates the machining zone, resulting in reduced friction. Fig. 20b Illustrates the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectra of the elements (Fe, Mo, Cr, Ni) present on the surface of the crater, where these elements are attached to the surface of the tool. The turquoise area, which accounts for 46 % of the total, plainly indicates that iron (Fe) is the predominant element in the tool's rake face. The development of BUL is primarily responsible for the extraction of work content. In addition, additional elements like as Mo, Cr, and Ni have a little contribution, as they were also present in the workpiece utilised during machining.

There was a notable reduction in the occurrence of chip particles sticking, chipping, and abrasion (Fig. 22 a) compared to OSQL and DM. The contact angle (CA) of quantum dots is crucial in reducing friction. In general, a low CA is desirable for coolant to achieve optimal efficiency [69]. The investigation reveals that the angle of the quantum dots (19°) is smaller than that of the acculube oil (42.7°). Quantum dots have the property of sliding and rolling mechanisms. Therefore, it tends to slide in the cutting part, which greatly develops the protection, so the friction is regulated [25]. The CD functions as a protective covering, preserving the tip of the inserts for a specific duration. The EDS examination with mapping spectra (Fig. 22 b,c) illustrate that significant quantities of work elements (Fe, Cr, Mo, Ni) are present, suggesting that the work portion is linked to the tool. However, the contribution of Fe, which accounts for just 56 %, is significantly lower compared to DM and OSQL. This indicates that CD has a significant influence on reducing tool wear.

The surface of the crater exhibits evidences of BUL as well as leftovers of chip material (Fig. 23 a) attached to the inserts. In carbon quantum dot (CD) machining, the reduced viscosity of the quantum dots fluid minimizes friction, leading to decreased built-up edge (BUE) and less chip material adhesion on the crater surface compared to dry and OSQL conditions. The elemental composition of the workpiece is determined using EDS analysis + mapping (Fig. 23 b,c), namely for Fe, Ni, and Cr. Similarly, the tool elements Ti and Al are also identified. It suggests that the level of wear in CD machining is lower compared to DM and OSQL. Additionally, the presence of the C element demonstrates the efficacy of carbon quantum dots in minimising tool wear by minimising friction in the chip-tool interface. Additionally, (Fig. 23 b,c), shows lower iron (Fe) adhesion (30 %) and identifies the presence of Ti, Al, and C elements, highlighting the effectiveness of carbon quantum dots in reducing tool wear and maintaining insert edges.

The use of cryogenic cooling (LN2) created a favourable cooling environment at the flank region, significantly reducing the temperature and minimising wear caused by friction. This led to a decrease in flank wear (Fig. 24 a) and greatly extended the tool's lifespan [76]. Furthermore, the cryogenic environment may enhance the strength of the tool. Additionally, it was noted that the occurrence of BUE and abrasion was reduced when compared to alternative cooling settings. LN2 machining lowers temperatures which in turn decreases thermal stresses and frictional wear on the cutting tools, potentially extending their lifespan and improving performance. The achieved lower temperatures reduce the thermal expansion of both the tool and workpiece, preventing deformation and improving overall tool durability. The further dispersion of the machined debris is partially inhibited. Over time, less wear results from this since there is less entrapment, which also reduces friction and tool abrasion. The intense cooling effect of LN2 can enhance the hardness and wear resistance of the cutting tool by reducing thermal softening during machining. This improved tool hardness helps maintain a sharp cutting edge and resist wear. The EDS analysis along with mapping (Fig. 24 b,c) conducted on the flank area reveals a significant presence of tool substrate (Ti, Al), which confirms that LN2 is highly effective in providing lubrication during machining. Furthermore, the primary component (Fe) present in the inserts was seen to be rather minor in comparison to the other cooling settings.

In this case, cryogenic machining effectively reduced wear on the rake surface [77] by minimising the friction factor among the tool and chip, while simultaneously enhancing heat transmission capabilities. The EDS measurements conducted on the rake face (Fig. 25 a) revealed a reduction in the presence of BUL and chip particles. Furthermore (Fig. 25 b,c), the contribution of the work element (Fe) is lower in comparison to the DM, OSQL, and CD conditions, indicating the effect of LN2 machining on the crater face during the turning of DSS.

The graph in Fig. 26 demonstrates that there is a direct correlation between the increase in cutting speed (Vc) and the enhancement of flank wear for all cutting situations. It is evident that an increase in Vc leads to an improvement in the hardness of the workpiece. Moreover, a rise in Vc obstructs the movement of coolant, leading to a reduction in the strength of the boundary layer formed at the tool-work area. Therefore, the elements stick to and remove the layer that is coated over the cutting tool, leading to an increase in wear on the flank surface. The observed flank wear rises proportionally with the increase in feed (f) across all cutting circumstances, as seen in Fig. 26. The high feed rate in this case leads to an increase in the metal removal mechanism, resulting in significant stress and a big quantity of heat being generated at the flank surface. As a result, the wear on the flank surface was seen.

The chip and its structural characteristics determine the surface quality, heat generated at the machining location, and the preservation of the tool tip. Therefore, by the examination of the chips' morphology, one may thoroughly study the process of metal removal and the failure of the tool. Observations were made of chips with large, protruding teeth (Fig. 27 a) that exhibited a dry state [70]. This condition was caused by extensive abrasion at the interface between the tool and chip, as well as plastic deformation. In addition, elevated temperatures combined with dry cutting create conditions that cause variations in the rolling surface of the chips. As a result, the chips exhibit a spring-like behaviour due to their bi-metallic structure, resulting in a more spiral-like shape. The front surface (Fig. 27a) experienced high temperatures, resulting in the formation of distinct solid lamella contours [70]. The bulky-teeth spacing (Pc) refers to the horizontal distance between two adjacent teeth. The P
C (Fig. 27a) was found to be high with dry condition [74–78] due to the divisions in the chip that separate from one other when fractures occur in the major deformation area, causing the front surface to expand significantly and leading to an increase in the P
C . The rear surface experiences significantly higher contact pressure and temperature compared to the front surface. This is because the plastic deformation of the back surface of the chip is restricted by the crater surface of the cutting tool. Friction marks were observed on the rear surface (Fig. 27b) due to intense rubbing in the cutting area caused by the absence of lubrication [78]. A significant quantity of primary dimples (Fig. 27c) has been detected, mostly caused by variations in temperature between the front and rear surfaces. Furthermore, the direct contact between the tool's crater surface and the chip caused the tool and work samples to adhere to the back surface, as seen in Fig. 27c. The EDS + mapping (Fig. 27c) of the chips' front surface reveals the coexistence of work material components and tool coating material.

Fig. 28 illustrates the SEM pictures of the front and rear surfaces of the chip. As the temperature increases in the deformation zones, the work component undergoes a transition to a ductile state specifically inside the shear bands [79]. Nevertheless, OSQL minimizes the plastic deformation on the front surface of the workpiece, hence contributing to a reduction in Pc. During the machining process using OSQL, a protective barrier formed in the cutting area, leading to a drop in temperature. This, in turn, facilitates the reduction of large teeth under OSQL circumstances [70]. The main serrations and lamella were slightly diminished with the use of OSQL, as effective lubrication was provided in the tool-chip area. The OSQL exhibited a lower reduction in the severity of friction marks (Fig. 28b) compared to DM. This can be attributed to the decreased friction resulting from effective lubrication in the tool-chip area [70]. In addition, just a little amount of the work material was stuck to the rear surface. To validate, the EDS together with mapping pictures (Fig. 28c) reveal the presence of the tool and work components on the front face of the chip, indicating that the Fe composition of 57 wt% is lower compared to the DM condition.

The front surface of carbon quantum dots (CD) exhibits a less prominent and protruding edge, as seen in Fig. 29 . Tooth patterns are typically observed as a result of the sliding flow of material being removed from the front area owing to significant plastic deformation. CD possesses a layered structure, causing the neighbouring layers to experience sliding due to shear stress, hence this leads to excellent lubrication [25]. Therefore, it decreases the size of the teeth in comparison to the DM and OSQL situations. The decrease in power consumption during the cutting process is caused by a reduction in frictional forces, which allows for the smooth movement of chips at a high speed. Therefore, the chips have a short transfer time as they pass over the tool's crater surface, resulting in weaker chips that are more prone to undesirable plastic deformation. Consequently, the pitch was steadily reduced. Relatively fewer little teeth were seen, which can be ascribed to the formation of a protective layer by the carbon quantum dots in the cutting area. Upon examination of the rear surface (Fig. 29b), it was noted that the quantum dots exhibited a low contact angle (CA). Fluids with low contact angle (CA) provide superior lubricating capabilities [57]. Therefore, the contact angle (CA) of quantum dots is discovered to be smaller than that of OSQL (acculube-2000), which effectively protects the layer at the machining point. Therefore, a reduction in friction marks is observed, as depicted in Fig. 29b. Nevertheless, a limited number of particles that stuck to the chips' surface were observed during CD machining. The EDS + mapping line scan (Fig. 29c) elucidates the influence of quantum dots detected on the back surface. The Fe 50.7 wt% major work component ingredient was significantly reduced compared to DM and OSQL. The quantum dots have the property of sliding and rolling mechanism. Hence it tends to slide at the cutting portion which significantly develop a shield, hence the friction was controlled [25]. Also, the structure of quantum dots seems to be spherical, which tends to change its shape to lamellar, and this phenomenon plays an important role in creating a clean sliding mechanism that reduces wear [25]. Therefore, it suggests that carbon quantum dot residues are effectively incorporated into the machining process.

The photos (Fig. 30 a) depict the fragmented front region of the chips, with the formation of bulky teeth being significantly reduced. LN2 has a significant impact on the chips, making them easier to separate. As a result, abrasion is reduced and a smooth surface is achieved. Additionally, the chips' fragility and their capacity to effortlessly glide away from the cutting area lead to the creation of a pristine region in the machining zone [6]. Nevertheless, it has been noted that the formation of initial serrations is a result of variations in shear stress and elevated temperatures inside the shear band. Chips are consistently moved from the tool/chip region to the neighbouring area and then cooled. Consequently, thermal stress was generated on the chips, leading to the creation of shear bands and subsequently the observation of lamellar structure [80]. A tiny friction mark was seen on the rear surface. This is due to the fact that the boiling point of LN2 is around −196 °C. When this coolant is supplied to the machining region, a decrease in temperature is seen. Consequently, a significant quantity of thermal energy is transferred from the surrounding environment [81]. Mapping with EDS (Fig. 30 b,c) studies revealed that just a little amount of tool material (Titanium and Aluminium) was found to be attached to the surface of the chips. The Fe contribution was 24.36 wt%, which was somewhat lower compared to other situations. This is due to the efficient lubrication facilitated by the use of cryogenic coolant.

The machinability index is influenced by the chip thickness and significantly affects the shear angle and power consumption of the machine tool [82]. When machining in dry mode, the high temperature generated causes the chip to adhere strongly to the cutting tool's crater face, leading to the removal of coated layers. Therefore, these variables contribute to an increase in chip thickness [83], as seen in Fig. 31 a. OSQL can effectively remove the chips from the tool-chip area by applying high pressure, which greatly reduces friction. Consequently, chips of smaller dimensions (Fig. 31b) were acquired [84]. During the initial phases, it was seen that CD had a spherical morphology. However, as a result of the ongoing machining process, a small number of CDs became stuck with both normal and shear stress, causing them to take on a lamellar structure. These geometries are capable of generating sliding when subjected to shear stress. Therefore, the rolling process of a CD lasts for a longer duration, thereby reducing friction [25]. Low friction is known to facilitate effective lubrication and the formation of a tribo-layer [25], resulting in the production of thinner chips as illustrated in Fig. 31c. The utilization of LN2 in machining reduced the friction between the tool and the chip by creating a protective barrier. Therefore, by lowering friction, it becomes possible to reduce the deformation of the chip and increase the cutting ratio [82]. Furthermore, it was noted that the chip breakability parameter was enhanced and a more effective control over the chips was achieved due to the high pressure exerted by the cryogenic medium. The reduction in chip thickness is evident as the cutting speed increases. Reducing abrasion in the tool-chip area leads to a decrease in C
t as depicted in Fig. 31d. During high-speed machining, BUE formations occurred. Nevertheless, these undesirable effects were eradicated when the Vc increased. Furthermore, a higher feed rate leads to increased friction, resulting in longer chips because to the observed increase in Ct.

The machining features of super duplex F 53 steel was explored with various lubrication conditions by experiments. Noticeable remarks are drawn, and the results are summarized below.
1)
As liquid nitrogen evaporates, it expands into gas, creating pressure that aids in removing chips and debris from the cutting area. This removal reduces their impact on the surface finish and leading to a smoother, more precise machining outcome. Thereby, LN2 significantly improves surface quality by rapidly evaporating and cooling the cutting area, resulting in an 82 % reduction in surface roughness (Ra) compared to dry lubrication, and exceeding the performance of OSQL and carbon quantum dots due to its superior heat absorption and elimination capabilities.
2)
The utilization of LN2 coolant significantly reduces temperature during machining due to its extremely low evaporation temperature (−196 °C). When applied, LN2 rapidly evaporates, absorbing substantial heat from the machining area, leading to temperature reductions of approximately 85 % compared to dry machining, 77 % compared to oil with small quantity lubrication (OSQL), and 58 % compared to carbon quantum dots (CD). Smoother surface finish and more accurate machining are the outcomes of the LN2 cooling procedure, which lowers thermal deformation and prevents heat build-up. This pronounced cooling effect minimizes distortion and oxidation, maintains a thin, stable lubricant layer, and further optimizes machining precision and surface quality.
3)
The extreme cooling provided by LN2 decreases the temperature of the cutting area, which reduces thermal stress and friction between the tool and workpiece. Partially the dispersion of the machined debris is prevented further. Due to the lesser entrapment, this lowers the friction and diminishes tool abrasion, leading to less wear over time. Consequently, there is an enhancement in the tool life. When using cryogenic coolant instead of dry mode, flank wear was reduced by 46 % making it prudent. With OSQL, this reduction dropped to 27 %, and with CD condition, it dropped to 15 %.
4)
Dry machining results in rapid tool wear due to high temperatures, evident from SEM images showing significant abrasion, tool breakage, and pronounced crater wear on the flank face. The absence of cutting fluid causes chip accumulation in the rake zone, leading to increased friction and built-up edge (BUE) formation and chipping in flank face. Whereas built-up layer (BUL) and (BUE) with removed coating were evident in the crater face of the tool. EDS analysis of both the features, confirms the adhesion of Fe, Mo, and Cr from the workpiece material on the tool insert, highlighting the adverse negative impact of high heat and friction on tool longevity.
5)
In this study, chip morphology analysis reveals that dry machining results in significant chip protrusions and high friction, leading to increased flank wear and built-up edge (BUE). OSQL improves surface quality by reducing chip teeth size and friction through effective lubrication, though some BUE and chip material remain. Carbon quantum dots (CD) further enhance chip flow and reduce wear by providing superior lubrication, resulting in fewer chip teeth and less adhesion. Cryogenic cooling with LN2 offers the best performance by minimising chip deformation, reducing friction and wear, and maintaining a cleaner machining zone due to its efficient cooling and lubrication properties.
6)
Microstructure analysis reveals that dry machining primarily preserves austenite with reduced ferrite grain presence, leading to increased hardness. In contrast, OSQL, CD, and LN2 cooling methods significantly enhance ferrite grain growth due to lower temperatures, reducing hardness. Among these, MQL results in the largest ferrite grains and lowest hardness due to increased thermal softening. During microstructure investigation, the ferrite grains show a slight increase in size as the temperature of the samples rises, except in dry mode and while lubricated. As a result, the use of LN2 produced lower hardness values since the samples became slightly softer than in other circumstances.
7)
Dry machining generates higher residual stress due to elevated temperatures, while OSQL, CD, and LN2 cooling methods effectively reduce residual stress. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis reveals that machining conditions do not significantly alter the phase composition of Duplex F53 steel, which remains predominantly ferrite and austenite. Cryogenic cooling improves grain quality, evidenced by increased peak size in XRD patterns, indicating enhanced crystallographic structure and reduced dislocation density.
8)
Among the selected parameters, the current comprehensive study concludes that cryogenic chilling using LN2 is the most suitable method for enhancing the machinability performance of duplex steel during turning operations.
M.Naresh Babu: Methodology. V. Anandan: Formal analysis. Ruby Thomas: Supervision. M. Dinesh Babu: Visualization. Sudhansu Ranjan Das: Formal analysis. N.L. Parthasarathi: Formal analysis.
